findings.title: Critical Accessibility Gaps for Visually Impaired Users
findings.description: Visually impaired users are unable to navigate the course catalog and complete enrollment due to a lack of ARIA roles and keyboard support. This significantly impacts their ability to use the platform effectively.
assumptions: Visually impaired users rely on screen readers for navigation.
Accessibility compliance is essential for educational institutions.

evidence.excerpt: Screen reader cannot navigate course catalog or enrollment flow.
Quiz forms use custom radio buttons and checkboxes that are invisible to screen readers.
James uses JAWS screen reader and is enrolled in mandatory corporate training through LearnPath.

recommendations: Implement ARIA roles and keyboard navigation for all UI components.

findings.title: Lack of Video Accessibility Features
findings.description: The absence of captions, transcripts, and keyboard controls in the video player hinders access for deaf, hard-of-hearing, and motor-impaired users, violating multiple WCAG standards.
assumptions: Video content is essential for learning.
Accessibility features are required for compliance.

evidence.excerpt: Video player lacks captions, transcripts, and keyboard controls.
James cannot complete his required training courses due to the unusable video player.

recommendations: Add captions, transcripts, and keyboard controls to the video player.

findings.title: Color Contrast Issues Across UI Components
findings.description: 60% of UI components fail to meet WCAG AA contrast ratios, making it difficult for users with visual impairments to read content effectively.
assumptions: Color contrast is crucial for readability.
Accessibility compliance is necessary for user satisfaction.

evidence.excerpt: Accessibility audit found that 60% of text elements fail WCAG AA contrast ratios.

recommendations: Revise color schemes to ensure compliance with WCAG AA contrast ratios.

findings.title: Need for VPAT for Compliance and Procurement
findings.description: The absence of a Voluntary Product Accessibility Template (VPAT) is a significant barrier for potential clients in government and education sectors, impacting purchasing decisions.
assumptions: A VPAT is essential for compliance with Section 508.
Government and educational institutions require documented accessibility compliance.

evidence.excerpt: Government and education sector prospects require a Voluntary Product Accessibility Template (VPAT).
Emily's agency needs a VPAT before any purchasing decision.

recommendations: Conduct a full accessibility audit and create a VPAT.

summary: The analysis reveals critical accessibility gaps in LearnPath that affect visually impaired and motor-disabled users, including navigation issues, lack of video accessibility features, and color contrast failures. Additionally, the absence of a VPAT poses significant compliance risks for potential clients in regulated sectors. Immediate action is required to address these issues to enhance user experience and meet legal requirements.
